segments.text: Alright, well, uh, welcome to the Social Service
and Community Sleep Committee.
Um, thank you for being here this afternoon.
So, uh, so it's probably the, the normal, uh,
practice we have is just if you introduce yourselves, um,
and then take some time to, um, make some opening statements
and then we'll go from there to questions members.
Great. Um,
so I'm Aaron.
I'm the CE of the Independent Children's Monitor.
And on my right, please,
Helen.
I'm the, uh, chief Monitor at the a
And on my left Tyson,
I'm the General manager,
corporate strategy and Insights at al.
Thank you. Just in terms of introductory, um, remarks,
um, this was our second
and our final year as a departmental agency.
And it was one of consolidation things change.
Um, our monitoring of the whole
of the Ana TAM system culminated in the publication
of our first system wide report.
So previously our focus had been,
and our mandate was on, um, the quality of TAM HU in Care.
Um, last year we published our first system wide report,
which looked at the whole of the Ang Tamari system
for Tamari, uhhi, Maori, and au.
Um, and then the final stage of our evolution
as an organization was completed on the 1st of August, 2025,
when we became an independent Crown entity.
And it was always the voices, um,
of those in the system at the center of our work.
Um, and in that last financial year,
we heard from more than 1,200 TAM caregivers
and professionals who support them.
So over the now five years, we've heard from over 6,000,
um, individuals about their experience
of care in the wider system.
Um, our outcomes from Maori report that I mentioned
before was our first of these reports,
and they will be delivered annually
as required by our legislation.
Um, a significant milestone in doing this.
Um, and in developing the report, we worked, um,
and received guidance from our Maori advisory group to kahu
as well as from Iwi, including strategic partners.
Um, as it was important that we, uh,
focused on the most important data
and information that we could to help influence change.
We also partnered with the Social Investment Agency, um,
seconded their staff to enable us to access data
that sat in the stats IDI so
that we could tell outcome measures for both, um,
Ranta at the age of 18, but then also young adults.
Um, and by putting those markers in place
and returning to them, we hope to see success in terms
of improved outcomes for young people
that are involved in the system.
Um, in February, 2025, um,
we published our fourth experiences of care report in roa,
and we are in the process of finalizing our fifth, um,
these care standards took effect in 2019.
And the lack of progress in complying with these, um,
highlighted by our 2025 report, um, led to the introduction
of a change to the oversight
of the Tamaki System Amendment Act, oh, sorry.
ACT. And the amendment bill brought in a requirement
that agencies now have to update their ministers
after four months of publication of
that report on progress they're making
with compliance and those standards.
Um, we also, in August 24, we published our review
of the implementation of the recommendations
that Dan Karin Potassium made following the death
of Young Malachi.
Um, and our report was, um,
more than 18 months on from that report.
And unfortunately, as I've probably spoken
to you in the past, the conclusion was that at
that time, children were no safer.
Um, because none of the recommendations had been acted upon
apart from one, um, which was us to come back
and review progress against those recommendations.
Um, we are now in the process
of completing our second report, uh,
following up on those recommendations.
Um, and it was through the drafting of that process
that government has, um, now accepted all
of Dame Karin's recommendations.
Um, and we are just waiting on, um, updates in terms
of further advice that is going to cabinet on implementation
of those recommendations so
that we can include them in our report.
Um, and that report will be published, um,
in February three.
Um, alongside those national-wide reports, we continue
to influence, um, change one community at a time.
Um, after each of our community monitoring visits,
we share back what we heard.
Um, our community share back is
provided to government agencies as well as iwi Maori
and community organizations that we meet with.
And they're published on our websites.
Um, these share backs are well received.
We survey attendees, um,
and 89% of survey respondents saying it is useful in terms
of their work, um, for us to, um, share back to them.
And 59% telling us they've already made
or plan to make changes
as a result of our monitoring visits.
Some of these changes include collaborating better
with other organizations, listening to Ranga Tahi voice
reducing silos and prioritizing training.
And then finally, um, in terms of the oversight system, um,
together with Una, um, the Children's Commissioner

of the oversight of the system
and collaborate on issues of importance.
Um, as an organization, our own organization,
we have a culture of continuous improvement, um,
whether it is in our monitoring practice, how we share back,
how we analyze information or how we deliver our reports.
Um, and, um, for the organization, um,
the recent public service, um, census demonstrated
that we're an organization
that are highly engaged in its purpose, innovative,
collaborative, respectful,
and I wanna thank all for their caring commitment they've

Just do we get that how urgent this is and
therefore how brave would it take?
What what would it take to actually blow it,
blow something out and go, go now go big and go early.
Yeah. I think probably
what you'd hear from Ang Tamari there probably is
there is a focus on this now and, and yeah.
And that is hopeful. Um,
but it is, um, it's a fundamental problem.
And I think when you think about the Ang Tamari Act itself,
um, the chief executive has an obligation to care
for Tamari and his kids.
Yes. Um, he can rely on services from other government
agencies and hope that they step up
and prioritize the delivery of those services for these kids
for the very reasons that we know.
However, the act also says if he's unable
to actually access those services,
that there's an obligation on him to pay for them.
And that's a challenging position to sit in. Boy.
And perhaps we need greater clarity in terms
of the obligations that sit on other agencies
to prioritize these kids.
Because until that happens, yes,
maybe we won't necessarily see
the progress that we all want.
And equally, if you think about the key standards,
those are standards that are all placed quite rightly on Ang
Tamari as the de facto parent quite right.
But there aren't obligations on education and health
and others to also deliver to young people that are in care.
Mm. And perhaps that's something that needs to change too,
to try and cut through some of the issues
that are being faced at the moment.
Well thanks. You left us on
an interesting point to contemplate.
Um, thank, thank you for time. This a very much.
